coredata.dc:title: Developing a robust treatment of antibiotics removal using Bi2O3/Cdots synthesized via microwave-assisted precipitation optimized by central composite design

coredata.dc:description: This study aims to optimize the development process of bismuth oxide/carbon dots (Bi2O3/Cdots) using microwave-assisted precipitation for antibiotics removal. Experimental design and analysis of optimum formulation predictions is conducted using the response surface methodology-central composite design (RSM-CCD). Analysis of variance (ANOVA) is used for model development and the fit optimized model is validated through actual experiments considering error estimation metrics. Optimal synthesis conditions is achieved by developing Bi2O3/Cdots 13.31%wt under 780 W microwaves for 17.35 minutes. The analysis of optical properties and crystallinity shows that the composite has a narrow bandgap compared to Bi2O3 with a lattice spacing of 0.32 nm. The morphological and topographical profiles shows that the composite structure consists of Bi2O3 rods with wavy, irregular, and rough surfaces decorated with colonies of Cdots. The chemical state of the composite consists of Bi, O, and C which are distributed throughout the surface with a lower binding energy compared to Bi2O3. Through disc diffusion, composite reduced the inhibition zone, which means it is able to reduce the antibacterial activity and antibiotic efficacy. The optimized composite is able to degrade 76.6% ciprofloxacin (CIP), 73.3% cefadroxil (CEF), 54.4% amoxicillin (AMX), and 60.3% mixed antibiotics (MIX). Our findings indicated that the developed Bi2O3/Cdots is capable of robust removal in various antibiotic pollutants.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study aims to optimize the development process of bismuth oxide/carbon dots (Bi2O3/Cdots) using microwave-assisted precipitation for antibiotics removal. Experimental design and analysis of optimum formulation predictions is conducted using the response surface methodology-central composite design (RSM-CCD). Analysis of variance (ANOVA) is used for model development and the fit optimized model is validated through actual experiments considering error estimation metrics. Optimal synthesis conditions is achieved by developing Bi2O3/Cdots 13.31%wt under 780 W microwaves for 17.35 minutes. The analysis of optical properties and crystallinity shows that the composite has a narrow bandgap compared to Bi2O3 with a lattice spacing of 0.32 nm. The morphological and topographical profiles shows that the composite structure consists of Bi2O3 rods with wavy, irregular, and rough surfaces decorated with colonies of Cdots. The chemical state of the composite consists of Bi, O, and C which are distributed throughout the surface with a lower binding energy compared to Bi2O3. Through disc diffusion, composite reduced the inhibition zone, which means it is able to reduce the antibacterial activity and antibiotic efficacy. The optimized composite is able to degrade 76.6% ciprofloxacin (CIP), 73.3% cefadroxil (CEF), 54.4% amoxicillin (AMX), and 60.3% mixed antibiotics (MIX). Our findings indicated that the developed Bi2O3/Cdots is capable of robust removal in various antibiotic pollutants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics have been considered a crucial pollution issue for the past few years. Approximately 100,000 tons of antibiotics were released annually into the environment from various wastewater streams [1]. Furthermore, most wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) cannot effectively treat polar micropollutants such as antibiotics [2]. Consequently, these antibiotic residues were accumulated as water contaminants due to their poor biodegradability, even at low concentrations [3]. This condition was worsened due to the COVID-19 pandemic in recent years. In the early stage of COVID-19 treatment, 74.6% of worldwide COVID-19 patients have been prescribed with antibiotics to prevent secondary bacterial infections [4]. In fact, only 8.4% of them were diagnosed with the infection [5]. This inappropriate prescription of antibiotics in treating COVID-19 resulted in a critical increase of antibiotic waste in the environment.

Numerous efforts have been extensively performed to degrade antibiotic waste using efficient and green technology. Among several wastewater treatment techniques, the Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs), especially heterogeneous photocatalysis is considered one of the promising alternatives [6]. This technique uses highly reactive transition species such as hydroxyl free radicals (OH*) and superoxide anion (O2 •−) to oxidize wastewater into relatively harmless end products such as H2O, CO2, and inorganic anions [7] with high degree of efficiency [8]. Among various photocatalysts, bismuth-based photocatalyst like bismuth oxide (Bi2O3) has been known as a promising material for degrading organic contaminants like antibiotic waste [9].

Currently, some research have been utilized Bi2O3 photocatalyst due to its narrow band gap energy, strong visible light responsibility, thermal stability, low cost, and facile synthesis process [9–11]. Unfortunately, the high recombination rate of photoinduced positive holes and electron hinder its photocatalytic performance [12]. To overcome the drawback of using a single-component photocatalyst, the construction of heterojunction is widely considered a promising approach to enhance photocatalytic performance by promoting visible-light activity and efficient separation of charge carriers through bandgap adjustment [13–15]. Recently, semiconductor carbon dots (Cdots) have been applied in photocatalysts due to their efficient absorption of visible light, low toxicity, water solubility, and high biocompatibility properties [16–18]. Thus, Cdots can be a promising option to synergistically enhance the photocatalytic efficiency of Bi2O3 by forming a heterojunction.

Several studies have been conducted photocatalytic degradation of pollutants using bismuth-based Cdots hybrid systems. The BiOBr/Cdots composite showed higher dye degradation efficiency than pure BiOBr [19]. A similar result was also reported by another study that developed the C-dots/BiOI composite and evaluated its photocatalytic activity [20]. The current hybrid composite that is being intensively developed is Bi2O3/Cdots, which is able to produce a higher photocatalytic effect to degrade various pollutants, such as indigo carmine [9], rhodamine B [21], bisphenol [16], and antibiotics [17]. However, most reports used only one factor at a time (OFAT) synthesis to determine its degradation ability. On the other hand, optimum synthesis conditions are needed to obtain the highest possible degradation efficiency, but with the lowest possible energy, raw materials, and time.

The properties of nanocomposites are significantly influenced by the characteristics of their raw material, temperature, pH, precursors concentration, synthesis time, energy, etc. The potential influence of these factors can be examined using experimental techniques that involve varying one parameter while keeping others constant at different levels [22]. These conventional experimental methods typically require a considerable number of test samples, which can be time-consuming and may overlook potential interaction effects among process parameters, ultimately leading to reduced optimization efficiency [23]. To overcome these constraints, researchers utilize response surface methodology (RSM), a statistical experimental design approach where all factors are simultaneously altered across a set of experimental samples. Its objective is to decrease the number of experimental samples while optimizing results using the collected data, which can subsequently be utilized to construct empirical models [24,25]. These models establish a relationship between the response and the experimental variables. Primarily, this model simplifies the quest for an improved process response, a refinement that is confirmed through experimental validation.

In this study, Bi2O3/Cdots were prepared to photodegrade antibiotic waste, that are cefadroxil, ciprofloxacin, amoxicillin, and a mixture of these antibiotics. These antibiotics were chosen because they are the most commonly consumed by the public and can pollute the environment, thus requiring material optimization to degrade the contamination [26,27]. The Bi2O3/Cdots nanocomposite was synthesized by a microwave irradiation method with optimizing various factors, including reactor power, synthesis time, and Cdots concentration. These factor were investigated to increase the effectiveness of photocatalytic performance under UV light. This study improves research that has been reported previously, where Bi2O3 was synthesized by the same method with one factor and one antibiotic response [28]. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first Bi2O3/Cdots performance optimization study using central composite design (CCD) to investigate the interaction of selected factors (microwave-assisted synthesis) for optimum removal of antibiotics. The resulted model is analyzed to develop response surface plots to optimize the main variables with maximum pollutant removal capability.

Cdots were synthesized with citric acid monohydrate (Merck KGaA, p.a., ≥99.6%), urea (Merck KGaA, p.a., ≥99.7%), and ultrapure water. Bismuth (III) nitrate (Merck KGaA, p.a.), nitric acid (Merck KGaA, p.a.), and sodium hydroxide (Merck KGaA, p.a.) were utilized for the synthesis of Bi2O3. The antibiotic solutions were prepared from cefadroxil 500 mg (Hexpharm), ciprofloxacin 500 mg (Hexpharm), and amoxicillin 500 mg (Kimiafarma). The synthesis was carried out in a modified microwave extraction reactor (Sharp M223BK).

Synthesis of Cdots was prepared by a convenient microwave-ultrasound method based on the procedure described in our previous study [29]. Citric acid 2 g and urea 3 g were dissolved in 60 mL of distilled water and homogenized using a hotplate stirrer at 70 °C and 600 rpm for 15 min. The solution was then transferred to an ultrasonic bath at 40 °C for 20 min. Next, the resulting solution was exposed to microwave irradiation at 800 W for 40 min. After cooling to room temperature, very dark powders were obtained.

The synthesized Bi2O3/Cdots powder was characterized using various techniques. The absorption spectrum analysis was performed using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Double-beam UV–Vis, UH5300 Spectrophotometer). The crystallinity of the obtained samples was investigated with X-ray powder diffraction instrument (XRD, Shimadzu Maxima-700, Cu 1.54 Å). The surface morphology structure and chemical composition were determined using scanning electron microscopy and energy dispersive X-ray mapping (SEM-EDX, JEOL JSM-6510LA). Topographic and surface roughness analysis of the Bi2O3/Cdots composite was performed using atomic force microscopy (AFM, Park System NX10). Particle size and lattice distance materials were obtained using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (JEOL 2100F HR-TEM operating at 200 kV accelerating voltage). The surface chemistry of materials was characterized using X-ray photo-electron spectrophotometry (XPS, Al-Kα excitation source hν = 1486.71 eV, Shimadzu Kratos Axis Supra).

The antibiotic removal experiment is depicted in Fig. 1(b). The removal process was commenced by preparing the antibiotics cefadroxil, ciprofloxacin, and amoxicillin dissolved in distilled water, each at a concentration of 100 mg/L. The combined antibiotic solutions were then mixed and stirred in a completely dark room for 90 min to reach equilibrium. Subsequently, 100 mL of the antibiotic solution was mixed with 25 mg of the Bi2O3/Cdots powder. The solution was placed in a dark box for 90 min, followed by exposure to UV light in a removal reactor for 180 min. The samples were observed and their absorbance was measured every 30 min to analyze the effect of the photocatalytic process on the optical properties. The treated samples were analyzed using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The photocatalytic efficiency (Ef) was calculated using Eq. (1), where Co and Ct represent the initial and final concentrations:

To ensure the efficacy of the method for antibiotics removal, the study ended by observing alterations in antimicrobial activity within samples before and after treatment using the disc diffusion test method.

Prior to constructing a model, it is essential to conduct design evaluations to assess the adequacy of the generated experimental design [30]. The outcomes of the design evaluation, which include the fraction of design space (FDS) chart, perturbation chart, a contour plot of the standard error of design (SED), and a surface plot of SED, are compiled in Fig. 2 . It can be observed that the FDS (Fig. 2 (a)) plot reveals a mean minimum standard error of 0.43 and a maximum value of 1.97, with an average mean standard error of 0.732. This indicates that the developed design is well-suited for optimization, considering that the average mean standard error value is below one. Moreover, the perturbation curve highlights (Fig. 2 (b)) that all variable values hold equal significance in the design space, implying that this design matrix enables accurate predictions. Upon analyzing the distribution of standard design errors in the contour (Fig. 2 (c)) and surface plots (Fig. 2 (d)), it is evident that the design region predominantly falls within the low-error range (0.5–0.8 σ) and maintains a relatively uniform distribution [31]. Based on the results of the design evaluation, all parameters meet the requirements for establishing a precise experimental design tailored for optimization purposes.

The experimental results based on the developed design of the experiment, including the number of runs, factors, and responses, are summarized in Table 2 . Furthermore, the experimental design outcomes outlined in Table 3 undergo rigorous statistical analysis employing the ANOVA method. This analytical approach is applied to generate regression equations that comprehensively account for all the examined responses. As a culmination of this analytical journey, the best-fitted empirical models for CIP removal, CEF removal, AMX removal, and MIX removal are expressed in Eqs. (2)–(5), respectively.

From the empirical models, the result can be classified into the two-factor interaction model (2 FI) for the response of CIP and CEF removal and into the quadratic model for AMX and MIX removal. To verify the accuracy of the models, the actual experimental dataset and verification dataset were compared with respect to the predicted value from the model, as the comparison results are shown in Fig. 3 . The actual experimental dataset is the data from the experimental runs as previously shown in Table 2, was visually symbolized by the blue rectangular shapes in the figures. Meanwhile, the verification dataset is the data from the verification experiment at some random points, in this occasion, the experimental run numbers 2, 6, 10, 11, and 15 are chosen. The verification experiment results are shown in the red circle shapes in the figures. The prediction lines are generated from the developed empirical models to show the variability of experimental data against the predicted values. Fig. 3 shows that all responses exhibit a good fit as suggested from the coefficient of determination (R2) values [32]. All responses have the R2 higher than 0.9. Moreover, the finding was also strengthened by the verification data, which fall near the predicted values. This indicates the least variability of data, suggesting that the models are desirable for accurate prediction and optimization [33,34].

In the statistical analysis, the significance of the developed empirical models, the independent factors, and their interactions have been extensively evaluated through ANOVA (F-value and p-value) [34,35]. The ANOVA results based on p-values for the models, factors, and interactions of all response variables are presented in Table 3. It is noteworthy that a p-value lower than 0.05 signifies a significant impact on the response and conversely [36]. Table 3 indicates that the p-values for CIP, CEF, AMX, and MIX removals are 0.0030, 0.0003, <0.0001, and <0.0001, respectively. All the p-values for the models are below 0.05. Consequently, all the chosen models exert a significant influence on the data prediction process.

The 2FI model demonstrates a significant effect in CIP and CEF removal, whereas AMX and MIX removal are depicted by the quadratic model. On closer examination, not all factors and interactions carry significance in every model. For instance, the two-way interaction of Bi2O3/Cdots concentration and time (x
1
x
2 ) shows no significant impact on CIP removal (p-value = 0.5635). In the context of CEF removal, a single factor of Bi2O3/Cdots concentration, the two-way interaction of (x
1
x
2 ), and the interaction of time and reactor power (x
2
x
3 ) are considered to lack significant effects. Similarly, for AMX and MIX removal, insignificant effects are detected for the single time factor and the two-way interaction of (x
1
x
2 ), respectively. However, by evaluating holistically, it's apparent that the number of significant factors outweighs those with insignificant effects. Consequently, these ANOVA results signify the importance of the empirical models, their respective factors, and their interactions. As a result, they can be appropriate for the purpose of prediction and optimization.

The developed empirical models for response surface plots are evaluated with regard to the model adequacy parameters: coefficient of determination (R2), adjusted R2, predicted R2, precision adequacy, pure error, standard deviation, and coefficient of variation (CV) [36,37]. As indicated by Table 3, the R2 values are determined as 0.9310, 0.9312, 0.9989, and 0.9956 for CIP, CEF, AMX, and MIX removal, respectively. All of these R2 values exceed 0.9, signifying strong fits [38]. Additionally, the adj-R2 and pred-R2 values for all responses are quite good. For instance, the pred-R2 value of AMX removal (0.9832) is the best compared to other responses, which suggests that the empirical model for AMX removal is effective in predicting approximately 98.32% of new data variabilities. Furthermore, the difference between adj-R2 and pred-R2 serves as a valuable indicator for model adequacy determination. A model is generally considered adequate when the value of (adj-R2 – pred-R2) is less than 0.2 [34,39]. For the CEF, AMX, and MIX removal responses, this difference stands at 0.1342, 0.0136, and 0.1904, respectively which are less than 0.2, thus means all those models are adequate. However, in the case of CIP removal, the (adj-R2 – pred-R2) value is calculated to be 0.2069, slightly exceeding 0.2. This suggests a relatively higher degree of data variability within the CIP removal model.

The precision adequacy values of models are found to be 13.87, 14.81, 63.50, and 30.53 for the CIP, CEF, AMX, and MIX removal, respectively. Precision adequacy is considerably desired when the value is higher than 4.0 [36], thus all models are considered adequate. Moreover, based on the values of pure error and standard deviations of the models, all models have small pure errors and standard deviations. The coefficient of variability (CV) is also assessed, which stands for the model's data variability level based on the mean value of variability. The reliable model should have a CV value below 10% [40]. It is observed that all responses of CIP (7.99%), CEF (1.84%), AMX (1.86%), and MIX (3.39%) removal have CV values below 10%. Therefore, by considering all of the model adequacy parameters, it can be reasonably accepted that all response models are adequate and appropriate to be used in the prediction and optimization.

Fig. 4 compiles the response surface contour plots for CIP, CEF, AMX, and MIX removal. These plots are constructed based on the x 1 x 2, x 1 x 3, and x 2 x 3 interaction plots generated from the empirical models presented in Eqs. (2)–(5). To interpret the contour plots, the red regions denote relatively higher removal values, while the blue regions indicate relatively lower removal values. Analyzing the response surface plots reveals a similarity in surface profiles between the CIP and CEF removal group and the AMX and MIX removal group. This similarity arises from the utilization of the same model types, 2FI model for CIP and CEF removal, and quadratic model for AMX and MIX removal.

The response represented by the 2FI model illustrates that higher pollutant removal was achieved at relatively higher composite concentrations, longer durations, and increased reactor power. These trends are clearly depicted in Fig. 4(a–f). In contrast, in the response surface plots of a quadratic model, the red regions emerge at the center of the plots, indicating that enhanced removal performance for AMX and MIX occurs under intermediate conditions of composite concentration, time, and reactor power. Notably, the removal of AMX and MIX necessitates a more central set of conditions compared to CIP and CEF removal. Furthermore, as revealed by the response surface plots, the removal performance range varies for all responses: CIP (32.87%–75.48%), CEF (17.84%–67.69%), AMX (16.80%–54.73%), and MIX (20.48%–56.73%). These response surface plots serve as crucial considerations when establishing optimization criteria [41]. Based on the results and analysis, it is reasonable to assign equal weight to all three factors in the optimization criteria. Consequently, the optimization process should be conducted to determine the optimal conditions for Bi2O3/Cdots concentration, time, and reactor power in order to achieve the desired removal performance.

The optimization criteria are: (i) All independent factors are in range, and (ii) maximizing the response for all pollutant removals. The overall analyzed constituents that consist of analysis covers three independent factors and four responses are considered to attain the optimization criteria. Furthermore, the optimization was subjected to further analysis by evaluating the desirability of the optimization outcome. The mathematical expression for the desirability is presented in Eq. (6) [40].
(6)

where D shows the desirability score, δ symbolizes the desirability of each factor and/or interactions, and n is the number of responses.

The desirability score ranges from zero to one, with a higher score indicating that the factors and response values are closer to the optimization criteria. Fig. 5 illustrates the desirability scores for individual factors and two-way interactions. Specifically, Fig. 5(a–c) displays the factor desirability scores for composite concentration, time, and reactor power. Based on the results, the perfect desirability score (D = 1) is attained for all independent factors. Furthermore, the desirability scores for the two-way interactions are also notably high. For the composite interactions, such as concentration-time (x 1 x 2), composite concentration-reactor power (x 1 x 3), and time-reactor power (x 2 x 3), perfect desirability scores are also achieved. This perfect desirability score suggests that the developed response surface models and empirical equations possess a strong potential for reproducibility, resulting in consistent outcomes. Consequently, the optimization results can be reasonably accepted based on these findings.

The optimization results, validation experiments, and analyses are summarized in Table 4 . It is noteworthy that the optimized removal performance can be attained by employing a 13.31 wt% Bi2O3/Cdots composite concentration, a processing time of 17.35 min, and a reactor power of 780 W. The predicted optimized pollutant removal rates are 76.60% for CIF, 73.30% for CEF, 54.38% for AMX, and 60.26% for MIX removal. To validate these optimization results, a validation experiment was conducted with three replications. The validation experiment outcomes reveal that under the optimized process conditions, the average pollutant removal values are 71.91% for CIP, 67.30% for CEF, 56.24% for AMX, and 63.18% for MIX removal. Simple statistical analyses were employed to assess the deviation between predicted and observed values. The measured standard deviations are notably low for all responses. It is also in agreement with the small error values of CIP (4.97%), CEF (6.66%), AMX (1.28%), and MIX (4.50%) removal. The errors are still acceptable since they are all below 10%. In conclusion, considering all findings and analysis results, it is confidently affirmed that a highly satisfactory optimization result for the photodegradation process conditions of pharmaceutical pollutants using Bi2O3/Cdots composite via CCD has been achieved and experimentally validated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and crystallinity of Bi2O3/Cdots
ce:section.ce:para: Several characterizations were conducted to validate the actual properties of the prepared composite with the optimal formulation. Firstly, the optical characteristics and crystalline structure of Bi2O3/Cdots are depicted in Fig. 6 . It can be observed that the addition of Cdots to Bi2O3 leads to a reduction in the band gap energy of Bi2O3 from 2.8 eV to 2.42 eV, as shown in Fig. 6(a–b). The obtained energy range aligns with the findings of Sharma et al. (2019), where energy band gap values of Bi2O3 and Bi2O3/Cdots were reported as 2.7 eV and 2.49 eV, respectively [9]. The introduction of element C presents impurity levels in the surface state which causes a decrease in the band gap between the surface state and the valence band [42]. Another factor contributing to the narrowing energy band gap is the overlapping orbitals between Bi2O3 and Cdots atoms, causing an increment in the valence band and a minor decrement in the conduction band [43].

The low energy band gap effectively reduces the energy required for electrons transition from the valence band to the conduction band. As a result, the efficiency of charge transfer from photogenerated electron-hole pairs is increased, which raises photocatalytic activity when exposed to sunlight [44]. In the lower energy band, an increased release of electrons into the conduction band results in heightened hole production in the valence band. The interaction of these holes with water molecules leads to the formation of hydroxyl radical compounds (OH), which effectively decompose organic pollutants [45]. Hence, an increased hole production contributes to the improved efficacy of pollutant removal.

The X-ray diffraction pattern of Bi2O3/Cdots (Fig. 6(c)) represents a monoclinic phase with diffraction peaks at 2θ of 27.65°, 33.50°, 33.31°, 27.18°, and 28.27°, corresponding to lattice planes of (120), (121), (200), (012), and (112), respectively (COD – Inorg 96-101-0005). The high intensity and sharp diffraction peak at 2θ of 27.66° indicates that Bi2O3 has a good crystallinity and thermal stability [46]. The Software-based matching analysis (Match!) confirms that the diffraction reflection solely corresponds to the monoclinic Bi2O3 phase, affirming the catalyst's high purity [9]. Notably, the addition of Cdots induces a slight shift in the Bi2O3 diffraction peak, from 27.40 to 27.66°. This shift indicates a low concentration of Cdots within Bi2O3/Cdots composite. Comparatively, according to the Debye-Scherrer equation, the composite exhibits a smaller mean FWHM value and a larger mean crystal size than Bi2O3. Morever, larger grain sizes are associated with higher ionic conductivity [47]. This enhanced conductivity significantly influences the material's transport of electron-hole pairs, consequently contributing to heightened photocatalytic efficiency. A detailed analysis of the Bi2O3/Cdots crystallinity parameter is presented in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Topographical and morphological profile of Bi2O3/Cdots
ce:section.ce:para: The Bi2O3/Cdots composite's topographical characteristics were investigated through non-contact mode atomic force microscopy analysis. Fig. 7 (a and b) shows the profile of the particles at a surface area of 2 × 2 μm, where light areas indicate higher surfaces and dark areas signify lower surfaces [48,49]. Cross-sectional examination of particles exposed elevation fluctuations akin to peaks and valleys. The observed 3D topography is revealed coarse, and irregular sheets forming the agglomerated granules. These aggregates are prevalent across the material surface, posing challenges in identifying nano-islands and accurately estimating individual particle sizes [50].

The addition of Cdots on Bi2O3 produced a rougher surface compared to undoped Bi2O3. This statement is supported by prominent surface roughness parameters: average roughness (Ra) and root mean squared roughness (Rq), as well as minor surface roughness parameters: slope degree (Rsk), and line kurtosis (Rku). This assertion is supported by prominent surface roughness parameters: average roughness (Ra) and root mean squared roughness (Rq), as well as minor surface roughness parameters: slope degree (Rsk), and line kurtosis (Rku). All of these parameters can be observed in Fig. 7, which shows a surface roughness of 14.06 for Bi2O3 and 17.36 for Bi2O3/Cdots. A substantial increase in surface roughness is believed to increase the active catalyst surface area [48]. It becomes a valuable characteristic for applications such as photocatalysis, where a larger surface area mean an enhanced photocatalytic activity [51,52]. This also confirms that modification of material using dopants at certain levels can improve the structural profile of photocatalyst materials [53,54].

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) are shown in Fig. 7 (c) for Bi2O3 and Fig. 7 (f) for Bi2O3/Cdots. The morphology of undoped Bi2O3 exhibits a surface composed of even agglomerated irregular flakes. In contrast, the microstructure of the Bi2O3/Cdots composite displays elongated rod-like shapes with spiky powder across the surface. These Bi2O3 rods have an average length of 4.6 μm and a diameter of 0.7 μm, where the surface is covered with Cdots colonies. The presence of the spiky powder structure indicates that Cdots have adhered and dispersed effectively, preventing agglomeration on the Bi2O3 surface. This combined structural configuration enhances light adsorption accessibility, yielding more active sites (electron-hole pairs) that influence pollutant removal and decomposition activities [11,55]. Such an integrated structure not only expands the active surface area but also accelerates charge transfer, and amplifying photocatalytic activity [28,46].

Fig. 7(d–e) shows the TEM and HR-TEM of Bi2O3 consisting of nanosheet films and a lattice spacing of 0.32 nm corresponding to the (1 2 0) lattice plane. Its characteristics are similar to previous studies reporting Bi2O3 with irregular nanosheet structures [10,11]. Fig. 7(f–g) shows the TEM and HR-TEM structures of Bi2O3/Cdots. It can be clearly observed that the black spherical Cdots nanoparticles adhere tightly to Bi2O3. Cdots were observed to be evenly distributed over the Bi2O3 surface (marked by a yellow ring) measuring about 8 nm and a lattice spacing of 0.26 nm according to the lattice plane (1 0 0) [19]. The appearance of interfacial overlap between Cdots and Bi2O3 indicates that the two materials adhere strongly and form heterojunctions. The growth of heterostructures on a composite is beneficial in the photocatalytic process for photogenerated electron-hole transfer mechanisms.

Fig. 8 (a and b) shows the Energy Dispersion X-ray (EDX) spectra of Bi2O3 and Bi2O3/Cdots. The undoped Bi2O3 exhibits a mass composition of Bi and O, with a composition of 79.02 wt% and 20.98 wt%, respectively. In comparison, the chemical composition of the optimal formulation composite has different results significantly. The composites were observed to show a mass composition of Bi, O, and C at 76.74 wt%, 14.45 wt%, and 8.81 wt% respectively. Each element is fairly distributed on the particle surface as shown in Fig. 7 (i,j,k), where bismuth is represented in green, oxygen is represented in red, and carbon is represented in blue. These results confirm that Cdots are perfectly dispersed in Bi2O3, so that potentially increasing the oxidation reduction space.

To further analyze the surface chemical composition and chemical state of Bi2O3/Cdots, XPS measurements were carried out. The high-resolution spectra of Bi 4f, O 1s, and C 1s are shown in Fig. 8(c,d,e,f). Fig. 8(c) shows that the binding energies of Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 for Bi2O3 are located at 159 eV and 164.3 eV, while Bi2O3/Cdots are located at 158.8 eV and 164.1 eV. The separation energy between Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 is 5.3 eV, confirming the formation of Bi3+ in the compound [13]. The O 1s peaks attached with binding energies of 531.1 eV, 533.4 eV, and 535.7 eV can be attributed to the Bi–O, CO, and C–O bonds, respectively. From the peaks of Bi 4f and O 1s, it can be observed that the composite has a lower binding energy than Bi2O3. These results are consistent with similar studies reporting the red shift phenomenon in the Bi2O3 composite [14,15]. Furthermore, to prove the existence of Cdots, C 1s has been deconvoluted into three peaks with binding energies of approximately 284.8 eV, 285.5 eV, and 288.9 eV, which are ascribed to C–C/C–H, C–O, and CO bonds respectively [56,57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance of Bi2O3/Cdots and antibiotics removal
ce:section.ce:para: Ciprofloxacin (CIP), Cefadroxil (CEF), and Amoxicillin (AMX) solutions were prepared to serve as representative pharmaceutical wastewater for evaluating the photodegradation performance of the optimized Bi2O3/Cdots composite. Before initiating the photodegradation process, the Bi2O3/Cdots powder was introduced to the antibiotic solution to achieve the adsorption equilibrium. This mixture was kept in the dark and stirred continuously for 90 min, during which the reduction in absorbance was observed. Subsequently, the photocatalytic capability of Bi2O3/Cdots was evaluated by exposing the sample to UV exposure for 180 min. The progression of photodegradation for each antibiotic was analyzed every 30 min, such as encompassing the removal of CIP (Fig. 9 (a)), CEF (Fig. 9(b)), AMX (Fig. 9(c)), and MIX (Fig. 9(d)).

However, after this period, the adsorption removal did not exhibit significant changes, indicating that the sample had reached a state of saturation. Although not markedly significant, it can be observed that the presence of Cdots led to an improvement in adsorption performance. The Bi2O3/Cdots composite displayed greater adsorption efficiency than undoped Bi2O3 for all antibiotics: 18.15% for CIP, 17.50% for CEF, 15.00% for AMX, and 14.50% for MIX. After 180 min, Bi2O3/Cdots facilitated the removal of CIP, CEF, AMX, and MIX under UV light exposure, resulting in values of 71.91%, 67.3%, 56.24%, and 63.18% respectively. This starkly demonstrates that the photodegradation capability of Bi2O3/Cdots surpassed that of undoped Bi2O3, which achieved rates of only 62.76%, 54.34%, 45.82%, and 51.73% for CIP, CEF, AMX, and MIX respectively.

Fig. 9(e–f) provides a comparison between the removal capabilities of undoped Bi2O3 and Bi2O3/Cdots composites for three different antibiotics and a mixture of these antibiotics, depicted through concentration reduction over time. This experiment highlights that the presence of Cdots in the composite enhances the removal efficacy of antibiotics by Bi2O3. This removal efficiency can be attributed to several potential phenomena. Firstly, the antibiotics become entrapped through an adsorption process onto the Bi2O3/Cdots surface. This arrangement facilitates interactions between active radicals and antibiotic particles, leading to more efficient and rapid photocatalytic effects over shorter distances [58–60]. Secondly, the morphological and topographical attributes of Bi2O3 became rougher in the presence of Cdots. This enhancement is associated with the activation of new sites [48] expanding the interaction area with antibiotic compounds and thereby fostering the photodegradation process. Thirdly, the incorporation of Cdots into Bi2O3 maintains crystallinity and enhances optical properties by narrowing the energy band gap of the resultant composite. This enables the composite to absorb a wider spectrum of light, thereby amplifying photochemical reactions [61,62] and augmenting the efficiency of removal for all antibiotics. Lastly, the low binding energy of the composite facilitates electron transfer in photocatalytic reactions. The photocatalytic process of Bi2O3/Cdots is visually depicted in Fig. 1(b).

The degradation is initiated when UV photons hit the surface of the composite, the electrons in the Bi2O3 valence band become excited and move towards the conduction band, leaving holes in the valence band. Cdots embedded on the Bi2O3 surface accept and accommodate electrons from the Bi2O3 conduction band. This electron transfer to Cdots is a significant phenomenon distinguishing it from the photocatalytic reaction mechanism of undoped Bi2O3. This process extends the duration of electrons in a free state, significantly inhibiting the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Cdots contribute to recombination rate inhibition due to their carbon nanostructure and its high electron storage capacity [44,63]. The electrons react with oxygen to generate superoxide radicals (O2−), while the holes interact with water molecules (H2O) to generate active hydroxyl radicals (-OH*). These two radicals are exceptionally reactive and can break down molecules into simpler compounds.

The exceptional performance of Bi2O3/Cdots in eliminating antibiotic pollutants was convincingly validated through the microbiological testing using the disc diffusion technique. This method was employed to characterize the antibiotics and assess their diminished ability to inactivate the microbes before and after photodegradation using the Bi2O3/Cdots composite. Antibacterial activity was evaluated based on the inhibition zone (clear area) surrounding the disc paper, where Staphylococcus sp. bacteria was cultured. This bacterium serves as an indicator of aquatic environment fertility. The inhibition zone diameter indicates the sensitivity of the tested bacteria: a larger area signifies a greater antibacterial activity [64,65].

The observation of the inhibition zone before and after treatment can be seen in Fig. 9(a–d). Antibacterial activity against Staphylococcus sp. revealed distinct inhibition zone diameters for each antibiotic. Prior to any treatment, CIP, CEF, AMX, and MIX exhibited inhibition diameters of 21 mm, 20 mm, 23 mm, and 20 mm, respectively. These values indicate the potential of all antibiotics in either killing or inhibiting microbial growth. Following photodegradation, the inhibition zone diameters for CIP, CEF, AMX, and MIX narrowed to 5 mm, 6 mm, 10 mm, and 5 mm, respectively. This reduction in diameter signifies a decline in the antibiotics effectiveness in inhibiting bacterial growth [66]. It confirms a decrease in the antibiotic concentration within the solution volume or signifies the removal of the antibiotic structure due to the active radical photodegradation by Bi2O3. These outcomes validate the Bi2O3/Cdots composite's remarkable capability to eliminate various antibiotic pollutants present in water.

Development of Bi2O3/Cdots via microwave-assisted precipitation method shows a suitable model (quadratic and 2FI) with a high degree of agreement between the actual and predicted values (R2 > 0.9). Bi2O3/Cdots 13.31%w exposed to microwaves 780 W for 17.35 min showed improvements in characteristics: optical properties, crystallinity, surface morphology, topographical profile, element distribution, and binding energy. Referring to physical and microbiological parameters, Bi2O3/Cdots convincingly has a robust ability to degrade various antibiotics, such as ciprofloxacin, cefadroxil, and amoxicillin. In future research, it is necessary to modify the composite into a thin film to be able to degrade antibiotics repeatedly. Furthermore, it is recommended to analyze the mechanism of antibiotic inactivation microbiologically so that it can show the impact on environmental health completely.
